# Playdate Lua vs. Standard Lua {#sec-appendix-lua}

Playdate Lua is Lua 5.4 — same syntax, same semantics, same reference
manual — built into a runtime that removes the parts of the standard
library that assume a host operating system and adds a handful of
conveniences of its own. This appendix is the reference version of the
ground @sec-lua-lab covers by demonstration: if you arrive from desktop
Lua, this is the diff; if a listing in this book uses something your Lua
does not have, this is where it is accounted for. Everything here is
verified against the SDK 3.0.6 reference (*Inside Playdate*, §5
"Developing in Lua" and §7.1 "Playdate SDK Lua enhancements") and, where
the reference is silent, against the live runtime via the
`02-lua-lab` example, which prints the answers on screen.

## The base language

The dialect is Lua 5.4. Everything structural — integer/float
distinction, `goto`, bitwise operators, metatables, coroutines,
variable attributes — is present and behaves per the Lua 5.4 manual,
with one configuration difference that matters:

**Numbers are 32-bit.** The SDK reference states it in one line: the
runtime is configured to use 32-bit numbers, where desktop Lua defaults
to 64. Both number subtypes shrink: integers run from
-2,147,483,648 to 2,147,483,647 (and wrap around on overflow, as Lua
integers always do), and floats are single-precision, good for roughly
seven significant decimal digits. In practice this bites in two places.
Counters that grow forever are safe as integers (a frame counter at 30
fps takes over two years to exhaust 31 bits) but *not* as floats: a
single-precision float loses the ability to represent every integer
beyond 2^24^, so `t += 1` on a float silently stops counting at
16,777,216. And accumulated float arithmetic — adding `1/30` to a clock
thousands of times — drifts sooner than 64-bit habits expect, which is
one more reason the book's skeleton (@sec-modes) treats frame numbers,
not float seconds, as the authoritative clock. Printed floats also show
fewer digits than desktop Lua's, which is cosmetic, but can startle
when a debug print shows `0.3333333` where you expected fourteen 3s.

Variable attributes deserve a note because the book uses one of them
constantly:

- **`<const>`** works exactly as in standard 5.4 and the SDK's own
  documentation recommends it for the `local gfx <const> =
  playdate.graphics` idiom — the binding cannot be reassigned and the
  compiler may treat it as a constant (@sec-lua-lab).
- **`<close>`** (to-be-closed variables) compiles under `pdc` and
  carries standard 5.4 semantics. You will almost never want it: its
  natural use is deterministic cleanup of OS resources, and the
  resources it was designed around (`io` file handles) do not exist
  here. None of the sixty games behind this book use it.

## What is missing

The sandbox removes the standard libraries that reach outside the
program, and the loader machinery that assumes a filesystem full of
`.lua` files. @fig-lab-sandbox in @sec-lua-lab shows the runtime
confirming this live.

| Standard Lua | Playdate status | What to use instead |
|---|---|---|
| `require`, `package.*` | Absent | `import` (see below) |
| `dofile`, `loadfile` | No role — all code is precompiled into the pdx by `pdc`; there are no loose `.lua` files at runtime | `import` |
| `os.*` (time, exit, env, process) | Absent | `playdate.getTime()`, `playdate.getSecondsSinceEpoch()`, `playdate.getCurrentTimeMilliseconds()` |
| `io.*` (files, stdin/stdout) | Absent | `playdate.file.*` for files; `playdate.datastore.*` for save data (@sec-save); `print` still works and goes to the Simulator console |
| `print` | **Present** | — (but see @sec-harness on why per-frame printing distorts timings) |

: The removed standard libraries and their Playdate replacements. {#tbl-lua-missing}

What survives of the standard library: `coroutine`, `math`, `string`,
`table`, and `utf8`, plus base functions like `pairs`, `ipairs`,
`pcall`, `select`, `tostring`, `tonumber`, `assert`, and
`setmetatable`. That set is your standard library; if a desktop Lua
snippet uses anything else, that is the porting work. When bringing
code in from elsewhere, grep it for `os%.` and `io%.` first — those two
prefixes account for nearly all of it.

## `import` is not `require`

Files join a Playdate game through `import "name"`, and it differs from
`require` in every way that matters (@sec-lua-lab demonstrates each):

- **One shared global environment.** There is no per-module scope.
  A global assigned in any file is visible to every file. Module
  structure is therefore a *convention* — this book's is
  module-per-concern: one file, one global table (`Game`, `Draw`,
  `Input`…), everything else `local`.
- **Each file runs exactly once.** A second `import` of the same file,
  from anywhere, does nothing. You never guard against double-loading.
- **Return values are discarded.** The desktop `local M = require
  "mod"` idiom has no equivalent; a file that ends in `return M`
  silently exports nothing.
- **It is resolved at compile time.** `pdc` walks the import graph and
  compiles everything reachable from `main.lua` into a single
  `main.pdz`. You cannot compute a filename and import it at runtime.
- **Import order is the dependency mechanism.** Files execute in
  import order; a file may read any global defined by files imported
  before it, including at load time.

The SDK also ships `import "CoreLibs/strict"`, which turns undeclared
global reads and writes inside functions into immediate errors — a
useful development-time antidote to the one-namespace spelling problem
(@sec-lua-lab).

## What is added

### Compound assignment operators

`pdc` extends the grammar with the assignment operators standard Lua
famously lacks (*Inside Playdate* §7.1):

| Operator | Meaning |
|---|---|
| `+=` `-=` `*=` `/=` | add, subtract, multiply, divide |
| `//=` `%=` | integer-divide, modulo |
| `<<=` `>>=` | shift left, shift right |
| `&=` `\|=` | bitwise AND, bitwise OR |
| `^=` | exponent (**not** bitwise XOR — Lua's `^` is power) |

: Compound assignment operators accepted by `pdc`. {#tbl-lua-ops}

They are pure syntactic convenience with one operational caveat: source
that uses them no longer parses in desktop Lua tools — linters,
formatters, test runners. The shipped games use them freely; code
meant to be shared with non-Playdate Lua should not.

### Table additions

The SDK extends the `table` library (§7.1):

| Function | What it does |
|---|---|
| `table.create(arrayCount, hashCount)` | New table with array and hash parts preallocated. The performance tool of the set: growing a table element-by-element in a hot loop is a GC tax you can pay once, up front (@sec-perf). |
| `table.indexOfElement(t, element)` | First index of `element` in an array-style table, or `nil`. Linear search — fine for menus, wrong for hot loops. |
| `table.getsize(t)` | Returns `arrayCount, hashCount` — the two parts of the table, sized separately. |
| `table.shallowcopy(source, [destination])` | Copy one level; nested tables are shared by reference. |
| `table.deepcopy(source)` | Recursive copy, including nested tables. |

: SDK additions to the `table` library. {#tbl-lua-table}

### The `json` global

A `json` table is available everywhere, no import required:
`json.decode(string)`, `json.decodeFile(path)`, `json.encode(table)`,
`json.encodePretty(table)`, and `json.encodeToFile(...)`. In this book
it appears mostly by implication — `playdate.datastore` (@sec-save)
speaks JSON on disk, which is why the harness heartbeat of @sec-harness
can be read by a shell script polling with `grep`.

### `class()` and Object, from CoreLibs

Not a language extension but easy to mistake for one: `import
"CoreLibs/object"` defines a global `class()` function implementing a
single-inheritance object system in plain Lua.

```lua
class("Oak", { rings = 0 }).extends(Tree)  -- properties optional

function Oak:init(age)
    Oak.super.init(self, age)   -- chain to parent; note .init(self, ...)
end

local oak = Oak(12)             -- instances: call the class
oak:isa(Tree)                   -- true
oak.className                   -- "Oak"
```

Every class inherits from `Object`; `ClassName.super` names the parent.
The one syntactic trap is documented by the SDK itself: chain to the
parent initializer as `Oak.super.init(self, ...)` — the colon form
`Oak.super:init(...)` passes the *class* as `self` and misbehaves.
@sec-lua-lab weighs when `class()` earns its keep against plain
tables; the SDK's sprite system (@sec-sprites) is the one place this
book *requires* it, because sprite subclasses extend
`playdate.graphics.sprite`.

## Runtime conventions worth internalizing

Three habits of the platform, all from the SDK's own conventions
section, that read as style but function as law:

- **Arrays are 1-indexed**, here as everywhere in Lua, and the entire
  `playdate.*` API agrees.
- **Dot calls tables, colon calls instances.** `playdate.graphics`
  functions are called with `.`; object methods with `:`. Confusing
  them produces the platform's signature hard-to-track bug
  (@sec-lua-lab).
- **Some APIs return objects, others return value lists** — e.g.
  `playdate.geometry.rect.new(...)` returns a rect object while
  `rect.fast_intersection(...)` returns four numbers. Check the
  reference before destructuring.

And one habit of this book that is *not* SDK law but follows from the
32-bit, GC-taxed runtime: cache `playdate.graphics` (and any other
namespace you touch per-frame) in a file-local `<const>`, keep per-frame
allocation near zero, and let @sec-perf show you what those two rules
are worth in milliseconds.
